one_line_takeaway: By certifying randomized smoothing through the dual (privacy-profile) view of differential privacy instead of the primal (f-DP) view, the paper obtains provably tighter, numerically tractable, and modular certificates against joint training+inference backdoor attacks, empirically improving DP-SGD poisoning certification over RDP-based baselines on MNIST/CIFAR-10.
key_finding: 1) The decomposition-based dual certificate (Theorem 4.1) is provably tighter than the non-decomposed primal approach of Lyu et al. [46]: for a subsampled Gaussian mechanism under 100 training-set changes, the pointwise minimum over decomposed tradeoff functions lies strictly above (i.e., gives stronger robustness guarantees than) the tradeoff function computed for the non-decomposed relation (Figure 2, illustrating a 'convexification gap'). 2) On poisoning-only certification (Figures 3-4, CIFAR-10 and MNIST, DP-SGD at epoch 8), the paper's 'DP-SGD (Privacy Profile - Numerical)' accounting achieves higher certified accuracy across radii than both DPA-based certification and RDP-based accounting (both the original 'Antidote' RDP baseline and their own 'corrected' RDP baseline), and also outperforms a 'Gaussian approximation' variant of their own privacy-profile approach. 3) The framework is shown to produce non-trivial joint (backdoor) certified-accuracy curves for combined training+test perturbations that no prior method could certify: certified accuracy vs. training radius R is reported for multiple inference-time L2 perturbation magnitudes δ (δ=0.05, 0.075, 0.1 for CIFAR-10 in Figure 5; δ=0.075, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2 for MNIST in Figure 7), comparing 'DPA + Gaussian' (100/130/150/200 partitions) against 'DPSGD + Gaussian, Privacy Profile Numerical'; and certified accuracy is also shown vs. the number of perturbed (rather than added/removed) training examples for various δtrain/σ ratios at fixed test perturbation δtest=0.2 (Figure 6, MNIST, DP-SGD with input noise σ=0.2, 8 epochs). 4) For DPA composed with an arbitrary base mechanism, Theorem 5.1 gives an exact closed-form privacy profile δc(ε) = R/N + (1-R/N)δb(ε), letting DPA's poisoning robustness be combined analytically with any inference-time certificate.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a framework for provable, certified robustness against backdoor attacks (joint training-time poisoning + inference-time evasion perturbations) by connecting randomized smoothing (RS) to the DUAL view of differential privacy (DP), i.e., privacy profiles / hockey-stick divergences, rather than the primal (f-DP / hypothesis-testing) view used in prior work (Lyu et al. [46]). The authors: (1) extend the primal-dual equivalence between f-DP and privacy profiles (Dong et al. [12]) to general, potentially asymmetric neighboring relations (their Theorem 4.3); (2) prove Theorem 4.1, a robustness certificate that decomposes a neighboring relation into sub-relations {≈ρi}, obtains a privacy profile δi(ε) for each, and certifies robustness by combining bounds derived from each δi; (3) show this decomposition is provably tighter than the non-decomposed primal approach of Lyu et al. (illustrated with a subsampled-Gaussian example under 100 changes, Figure 2, where taking the minimum over decomposed tradeoff functions strictly dominates the tradeoff function of the non-decomposed relation); (4) instantiate the framework for three concrete mechanisms: DP-SGD alone (poisoning-only certification, Example 1), DP-SGD composed with inference-time Gaussian noise (joint backdoor certification, Example 2), and Deep Partition Aggregation (DPA) composed with an arbitrary inference-time mechanism, for which they derive a closed-form privacy profile and tradeoff function (Theorem 5.1: δc(ε) = R/N + (1 - R/N)δb(ε)). Numerical composition of privacy-loss distributions (PLDs) uses the method of Doroshenko et al. [13] via the Google DP library [24].
threat_model: Backdoor/joint threat model: an adversary can simultaneously (a) perturb the training dataset Xtrain within a distance R under dtrain (e.g., up to R additions/removals/substitutions, or perturb up to R training examples by up to δtrain in L2 norm), and (b) perturb the test input xtest within radius ρ/δtest under an L2 (or general) distance dinput. The joint training-inference procedure J is defined (R,ρ)-robust if its prediction is invariant to any such joint perturbation. This is explicitly broader than pure evasion robustness (test-time only) or pure poisoning robustness (train-time only), which the paper treats as special cases obtained by fixing one side of the perturbation to zero.
limitations: Stated by the authors in the Conclusion/Limitations section: (1) Certifying DP-SGD requires sampling many models to estimate the relevant expectations, introducing non-trivial computational overhead, though they note this is an offline, parallelizable cost. (2) Certification via decomposition scales as O(R): for the subsampled Gaussian mechanism under R additions/deletions, R+1 distinct sub-cases (r+,r- pairs) must be certified, which the authors say remains tractable only for 'moderate threat sizes.' The paper does not report results for radii or model scales beyond MNIST/CIFAR-10-sized image classifiers and epoch counts up to 8 (with degradation over more epochs relegated to an appendix), suggesting scalability to larger models/datasets or longer training is untested in the main results.

quantitative_results: Figure 2: pointwise minimum over decomposed tradeoff functions (r+ + r- = 100) for a subsampled Gaussian mechanism (gamma=0.00256, sigma=0.05) lies strictly above the non-decomposed tradeoff function for relation ~100, demonstrating a 'convexification gap'

Figures 3-4: certified accuracy vs. radius (epoch 8) on CIFAR-10 (radius 0-60) and MNIST (radius 0-80) comparing DPA (100 partitions on CIFAR-10; 100/150/200 partitions on MNIST), DP-SGD with RDP 'Antidote' accounting, DP-SGD with 'corrected' RDP accounting, DP-SGD with Privacy Profile Gaussian Approximation, and DP-SGD with Privacy Profile Numerical accounting (the paper's method) -- the Privacy Profile Numerical curve is reported as outperforming the RDP-based baselines

Figure 5: joint certified accuracy vs. training radius (0-60) on CIFAR-10 for inference-time L2 perturbations delta=0.05, 0.075, 0.1, comparing 'DPA + Gaussian' (100 partitions, 10 epochs DP-SGD) vs. 'DPSGD + Gaussian, Privacy Profile Numerical'

Figure 6: certified accuracy vs. number of perturbed training examples (0-35) on MNIST for delta/sigma ratios 0.5, 1, 5, 10 at fixed test perturbation delta_test=0.2, DP-SGD trained 8 epochs with input Gaussian noise sigma=0.2

Figure 7: joint certified accuracy vs. training radius (0-60) on MNIST for inference-time perturbations delta=0.075, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, comparing DPA (100 and 130 partitions) + Gaussian vs. DPSGD + Gaussian Privacy Profile Numerical, 8 epochs of DP-SGD
baselines_compared: RDP-based accounting for DP-SGD ('Antidote', from Liu et al. [43])
A 'corrected' RDP-based accounting for DP-SGD derived by the authors themselves in Appendix F (to fix Liu et al.'s failure to account for sensitivity increase with radius R)
Gaussian-approximation variant of the paper's own Privacy Profile method (subsampled Gaussian approximated as a plain Gaussian with effective std sigma/gamma)
The primal (f-DP) certification approach of Lyu et al. [46], compared theoretically (not as an experimental curve) via the tradeoff-function tightness argument in Figure 2
